_comment: Copy to .fleet/ports.json (or run `fleet spec init`). This is the registry the `provides`/`consumes` ids in examples/fanout.example.json resolve against. Workflow: (1) write the frozen port — a TYPED artifact, never prose; (2) register it here; (3) COMMIT it to the base branch; (4) `fleet spec stub` so consumers compile on day zero; (5) declare provides/consumes in the fanout manifest; (6) `fleet spec check m.json` REFUSES (exit 2) a dangling consumes, two units providing the same port, or a cycle in the provides->consumes DAG. `fleet spec check` also runs inside `fleet_gate` as a fast PRE-GATE and fails any unit whose diff touches a port artifact it does not provide. It is NEVER the oracle: contract tests caught 41/53 (77%) of seeded integration defects (STVR 2025) and missed nearly every value-range bug — 'They could not replace the service black-box tests but only complement them.' The TEST SUITE decides correctness.

port:AST.description: The AST node definitions the parser emits and codegen consumes. A .d.ts, because the type-checker can prove conformance to it — for free, deterministically, and better than any reviewer.

port:KV.description: The key-value store interface. A .pyi: fleet is stack-agnostic — the artifact is whatever YOUR type-checker reads.
